summary: Tooling-only PR that replaced the shell/perl convert_hex helper with a standalone Python script, tightened duplicate/self-replacement handling in the Rust replace-symbols tool, bumped Rust tool crate versions, and added ldscript.lcf to rust-utils source-file glob tracking.
what_changed: Added tools/convert_hex.py as an executable Python 3 script that converts hexadecimal integer literals matching 0x[0-9a-fA-F]{1,7} with optional U suffix into decimal text.
Removed the old convert_hex shell function from tools/functions.sh, which had implemented the conversion through a perl -Mbigint one-liner.
convert_hex.py supports both in-place file rewriting and stdin/stdout operation when no file or '-' is passed.
Updated replace-symbols duplicate handling: replacements where from == to now emit a warning and are skipped.
Updated replace-symbols duplicate replacement handling so if a symbol is replaced more than once, the existing replacement is removed and the symbol is tracked in duplicates.
Added ldscript.lcf to the rust-utils SRC_FILES glob set alongside obj_files.mk.
smart_moves: Moving convert_hex from a sourced shell function to a standalone Python script made the utility easier to invoke directly and gave it clearer argument parsing behavior.
The Python replacement preserved the key regex behavior of the perl helper, including optional U suffixes and limiting matched hexadecimal digit length to 1 through 7 digits.
Supporting '-' and missing input as stdin allows convert_hex.py to be used both in pipelines and for in-place file edits.
replace-symbols now explicitly rejects self-replacements, avoiding no-op symbol map entries that could hide mistakes.

When duplicate replacements are detected, replace-symbols removes the prior replacement instead of leaving the first one active, which is safer because ambiguous replacement intent results in skipping rather than silently choosing one.
Adding ldscript.lcf to rust-utils source glob tracking suggests dependency/file scanning now accounts for linker-script changes, not just obj_files.mk and regular source files.
decomp_lessons: Small repository tools used during decompilation should prefer explicit scripts with argument parsers over hidden shell functions when the behavior needs to be reusable or pipeline-friendly.
Symbol replacement tooling should treat duplicate mappings and self-mappings as data quality problems; skipping ambiguous mappings is safer than applying a possibly wrong rename.

When build or analysis utilities cache or glob project inputs, linker scripts such as ldscript.lcf may need to be included as source-affecting files because they can affect output layout and matching.
Version bumps in tool crates are useful markers when behavior changes in command-line utilities, especially when downstream scripts may depend on exact replacement semantics.
naming_conventions: New Python helper uses snake_case names: convert_hex, process_input, input_file.
Rust duplicate-detection locals use descriptive snake_case booleans: replacement_exists and duplicate_exists.
Rust tool package names retain the melee-* prefix: melee-replace-symbols and melee-utils.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Hex literal normalization can help convert decompiler or source text constants from hexadecimal to decimal form while preserving only a narrow numeric-literal pattern.
Symbol replacement maps should avoid ambiguous duplicate entries before applying renames, because a mistaken symbol rename can damage matching work across generated or decompiled files.
Including ldscript.lcf in source glob tracking helps tooling notice linker layout changes that may affect object ordering, symbol placement, or build reproducibility.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify tools/convert_hex.py or change the hexadecimal literal regex.
Find later replace-symbols changes involving duplicate handling, symbol maps, or warning behavior.
Find rust-utils changes involving SRC_FILES, GlobSetBuilder, obj_files.mk, or ldscript.lcf.
Check whether scripts or documentation were later updated to call tools/convert_hex.py instead of the removed shell function.
